content.text: Platform teams collect friction signals from multiple sources — support tickets, error logs, survey responses, Slack threads, CI failure patterns. Without a structured system to aggregate, deduplicate, prioritise, and track these signals, the team ends up fixing the loudest complaint rather than the most impactful one. Friction tracking is the operational practice that converts scattered observations into a prioritised backlog where every item has a clear owner, estimated effort, and measurable outcome.

Friction shows up in different forms in different channels. Each source has a different signal quality: support tickets are explicit but under-count, while error logs are exhaustive but noisy. A complete picture requires all channels.
High — precise error type and call site; low false positive rate
High — exact failure; requires mapping key_id to team
Medium — rich context; represents only teams willing to file a ticket
Medium — covers silent failures; lagging indicator
High — captures integration failures; requires parsing job logs
Low — noisy; high signal when multiple people echo the same issue
Medium — indicates confusing or misaligned docs; requires correlation
Very high — documented failures with business impact

Every distinct friction point is recorded as a structured item in the friction backlog. The schema is designed to capture everything a sprint planner needs: where the signal came from, who is affected, what it costs them, and how hard it is to fix.

# friction_item.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class FrictionItem:
    id:           str            # auto-generated: friction-YYYYMMDD-NNN
    title:        str            # one line: what is the friction
    component:    str            # sdk | gateway | eval-runner | prompt-registry | docs | ci-pipeline
    severity:     Literal["blocker", "high", "medium", "low"]
    sources:      list[str]      # ["ticket-234", "survey-q2-2026-comment-12", "slack-2026-06-14"]
    affected_teams: list[str]    # team IDs; empty = unknown/broad
    signal_count: int            # how many independent signals point to this item
    user_impact:  str            # one sentence: what the developer actually can't do or has to work around
    root_cause:   str | None     # hypothesis; updated as investigation proceeds
    effort:       Literal["< 1 day", "1–3 days", "1–2 weeks", "> 2 weeks", "unknown"]
    priority:     Literal["P1", "P2", "P3", "P4", "wont-fix"]
    owner:        str | None     # platform team member
    status:       Literal["new", "investigating", "in-sprint", "fixed", "wont-fix", "known-limitation"]
    created_at:   date = field(default_factory=date.today)
    resolved_at:  date | None = None
    fix_pr:       str | None = None   # link to the PR that fixed it

    @property
    def priority_score(self) -> float:
        sev_weight = {"blocker": 4, "high": 3, "medium": 2, "low": 1}
        effort_weight = {"< 1 day": 4, "1–3 days": 3, "1–2 weeks": 2, "> 2 weeks": 1, "unknown": 1}
        reach = min(len(self.affected_teams) if self.affected_teams else self.signal_count, 10)
        return (sev_weight[self.severity] * reach * self.signal_count) / effort_weight[self.effort]

The same underlying friction shows up in multiple channels simultaneously — a confusing error message might generate three support tickets, two Slack threads, and five survey comments. Without deduplication, each signal is counted as a separate item and the true scale of the problem is hidden. The aggregation step merges these into one friction item and increments its signal count.
New friction items are triaged weekly by a rotating TC. Triage takes 30 minutes and covers all items with status
. The triager fills in the root cause hypothesis, effort estimate, and priority, then assigns an owner if the item is P1 or P2.
Prevents any team from using a platform component; data loss risk; security issue
Owner assigned and in-sprint within 1 business day
Affects ≥ 5 teams or the golden-path critical path; work-around exists but is painful
Affects 2–4 teams; work-around exists and is tolerable
Affects 1 team; cosmetic; minor convenience improvement
Considered during quarterly backlog grooming; may be deferred
Out of scope; by design; cost exceeds benefit; blocked externally
Documented with reason; closed with a comment so reporters know the outcome

# Friction Triage — [Date]

**Facilitator:** [rotating TC]
**Backlog:** https://github.com/internal/genai-platform/issues?label=friction

## Agenda

1. (5 min)  New P1 items since last triage — immediate assignment
2. (15 min) Triage new items (status = "new"):
   - Confirm component, severity, affected teams
   - Set effort bucket
   - Assign priority using P1–P4 rules
   - Name owner for P1/P2
3. (5 min)  Review stale P2 items (in-sprint > 3 weeks with no update)
4. (5 min)  Update quick-wins sprint: any P3/P4 items that are actually < 1 day?

## Output
- All "new" items move to "investigating" or "won't-fix"
- P1 items have an assigned owner
- Triage notes written to the GitHub issue
The Friction Backlog as a Public GitHub Board

The friction backlog is maintained as a public GitHub Project board visible to all engineers in the organisation. Transparency serves two purposes: engineers can see that their reported friction is tracked, and they can upvote items to signal additional reach without filing a duplicate ticket.

# Friction backlog conventions

## Issue labels
- friction:sdk          — GenAI LLM SDK
- friction:gateway      — LLM gateway
- friction:eval-runner  — Eval runner
- friction:prompt-reg   — Prompt registry
- friction:docs         — Documentation
- friction:ci-pipeline  — CI/CD templates
- friction:onboarding   — New-team onboarding
- priority:p1 / p2 / p3 / p4 / wont-fix
- effort:quick-win / sprint / project

## Issue template

**Friction summary:** (one line — what is the developer trying to do that isn't working)

**Signal sources:** (ticket numbers, survey quote IDs, Slack thread links)

**Affected teams:** (list of team IDs or "unknown")

**Signal count:** (how many independent reports)

**User impact:** (one sentence: what can't the developer do, or what do they have to do instead)

**Root cause hypothesis:** (leave blank if unknown)

**Effort estimate:** (quick-win / sprint / project / unknown)

**Proposed fix:** (optional — if the reporter has a suggestion)
P3 and P4 items with effort estimate

are batched into a monthly quick-wins sprint. One TC spends one week clearing as many quick wins as possible. This keeps the backlog from accumulating a long tail of small improvements that individually don't justify a sprint slot but collectively create significant friction.

# Quick-wins sprint — June 2026

**TC:** Jane Doe
**Period:** June 2–6

## Completed quick wins

| Friction item                           | Fix                                            | Time |
|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------|
| friction-20260510-004: Error message lacks guidance | Added "See docs.internal/genai/error-codes" to 8 error messages | 2h  |
| friction-20260517-002: CHANGELOG missing from PR template | Added CHANGELOG reminder to .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md | 30m |
| friction-20260520-009: Default timeout too short for large contexts | Changed default from 10s to 30s; documented in CHANGELOG | 1.5h |
| friction-20260522-001: Eval runner CLI help text missing examples | Added 3 usage examples to `genai-eval run --help` | 1h  |
| friction-20260528-003: Gateway docs link to old auth guide | Updated 6 docs pages with correct link | 20m |

**Total:** 5 items in 5.3 engineering-hours
**Announced:** In June digest and Slack #genai-platform-updates

Fixing a friction item is only complete when the fix is verified to have reduced the friction signal in production. Each resolved item must have a measurable success criterion defined before it is closed.
Error rate for this error type drops ≥ 50% in the 4 weeks after fix ships
Ticket volume for this theme drops ≥ 40% in the quarter after fix
Survey score for the affected dimension improves ≥ 0.3 points in the next survey
Bounce rate on the fixed doc page drops ≥ 20% in the 4 weeks after fix
P75 for the relevant milestone delta improves ≥ 20%
If a fix does not move the metric within the measurement period, the friction item is re-opened with the label
and a new root cause hypothesis is developed. This prevents the platform team from claiming credit for changes that didn't actually help.

Each signal source has a different access pattern. Rather than manually reviewing logs and tickets, the aggregation job runs as a weekly cron and pulls structured data from each source into a normalised signal format before calling

# aggregator/ingest_all.py
# Runs weekly; pulls from all signal sources and merges into backlog.json

import json
import httpx
from datetime import date, timedelta

PROMETHEUS_URL = "http://prometheus.internal:9090"
JIRA_URL       = "https://jira.internal"
JIRA_TOKEN     = "<vault:genai-platform/jira-token>"

def fetch_sdk_errors(since: date) -> list[dict]:
    """Pull top SDK error types exceeding threshold from Prometheus."""
    q = (
        f'topk(20, sum by (error_type, component) ('
        f'  increase(genai_sdk_errors_total[7d])'
        f'))'
    )
    resp = httpx.get(f"{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query", params={"query": q})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    signals = []
    for result in resp.json()["data"]["result"]:
        m = result["metric"]
        count = float(result["value"][1])
        if count < 5:
            continue
        signals.append({
            "title":     f"{m['error_type']} in {m.get('component','sdk')}",
            "component": m.get("component", "sdk"),
            "source":    f"prometheus-sdk-errors-{since.isoformat()}",
            "team":      None,
            "severity":  "high" if count > 50 else "medium",
        })
    return signals

def fetch_support_tickets(since: date) -> list[dict]:
    """Pull JIRA tickets tagged genai-platform-friction since the given date."""
    jql = (
        f'project=GENAI AND labels=platform-friction '
        f'AND created >= "{since.isoformat()}"'
    )
    resp = httpx.get(
        f"{JIRA_URL}/rest/api/3/search",
        params={"jql": jql, "fields": "summary,components,priority,reporter"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {JIRA_TOKEN}"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    signals = []
    for issue in resp.json()["issues"]:
        f = issue["fields"]
        component = f["components"][0]["name"] if f.get("components") else "unknown"
        signals.append({
            "title":     f["summary"],
            "component": component,
            "source":    f"jira-{issue['key']}",
            "team":      f.get("reporter", {}).get("displayName"),
            "severity":  {"Highest":"blocker","High":"high","Medium":"medium"}.get(
                f["priority"]["name"], "low"
            ),
        })
    return signals

def run_aggregation():
    since = date.today() - timedelta(days=7)
    with open("backlog.json") as fp:
        backlog = [FrictionItem(**item) for item in json.load(fp)]

    for signal in fetch_sdk_errors(since) + fetch_support_tickets(since):
        ingest_signal(signal, backlog)

    with open("backlog.json", "w") as fp:
        json.dump([vars(item) for item in backlog], fp, indent=2, default=str)
The lifecycle of one friction item illustrates how the system connects signal detection to verified resolution. The following example traces a real-pattern issue: teams hitting
because the default retry backoff does not account for per-minute token quotas on the gateway.

Prometheus shows 'RetryExhaustedError' in sdk rising from 3/week to 47/week over two weeks. The weekly aggregation job creates friction item friction-20260601-001 with severity=high, signal_count=1, status=new.

Three support tickets ("SDK keeps failing with RetryExhaustedError on high-volume queries") are ingested. Similarity score > 0.70 matches to the existing item. signal_count rises to 4; affected_teams gains 3 team IDs.
Weekly triage assigns P2. Root cause hypothesis: "Default backoff does not respect Retry-After header from gateway's 429 response." Effort: 1–3 days. Owner: @alice.
@alice confirms the gateway returns Retry-After but the SDK's exponential backoff ignores it. The fix is to parse the header and use it as the wait duration when present.
PR merged: SDK now reads Retry-After, falls back to exponential only when header absent. CHANGELOG entry added. Version bumped 1.4.0 → 1.5.0. Item status set to 'fixed', fix_pr set.
Four weeks post-fix: RetryExhaustedError rate is 3/week (down from 47/week peak). ≥ 50% reduction threshold met. Item closed. The improvement is noted in the quarterly DevEx digest.
The friction backlog feeds directly into quarterly roadmap planning. P1 and P2 items with effort estimate
that survive multiple triage cycles are nominated as roadmap projects. The nomination requires three pieces of evidence:
Signal persistence: item has been open for ≥ 4 weeks without resolution.
Reach evidence: affected_teams has ≥ 5 teams, or the item affects the critical path of a strategic initiative.
Cost estimate: TC provides a rough story-point estimate and names an owning team member who will lead the project.

Once on the roadmap, the friction item is linked to the quarterly epic in the project tracker. A roadmap slot commits the platform team to a fix within the quarter. Items that miss the quarter slot are re-prioritised (not silently deferred), and the miss is explained in the next monthly digest.
Treating the loudest team as the signal
Teams comfortable escalating dominate the backlog; quieter teams' friction is invisible
Weight by signal count and affected_teams size, not by who filed the ticket
Closing items when a fix ships
A fix that doesn't work in practice stays closed; the friction persists
Keep the item 'in-verification' until the success metric is confirmed by data
Support tickets, survey responses, and error logs are tracked in three different tools; duplicates are never merged
Single friction backlog with source references; ingest all channels into one schema
The backlog accumulates hundreds of P3 items that are never addressed; engineers stop reporting because nothing happens
P3 items with no action in 2 quarters are reviewed for wont-fix or reclassification
Fixes address the symptom rather than the cause; the same error type recurs under a different name
Root cause field required before an item moves to in-sprint; hypothesis updated as investigation proceeds

Platform friction tracking is the operational backbone that makes DevEx improvement systematic rather than reactive. By aggregating signals from all channels into a unified friction backlog — with a consistent schema, a weekly triage cadence, transparent public visibility, and verified success metrics — the platform team maintains a continuously prioritised list of improvements ranked by impact on developer productivity. Quick-wins sprints clear the long tail of small improvements monthly. The public backlog keeps the feedback loop visible and builds trust that reported friction is tracked and acted on. Measured success criteria prevent the team from assuming that shipping a fix is the same as reducing friction.
